ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 covers milk tea as a general category; no specific low-fat variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific low-fat milk tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 covers milk tea as a substance; no low-fat variant code exists. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: served chilled or at room temperature

serving.description: 1 medium cup (approx. 500 ml / 500 g as served)
serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of low fat milk tea as typically served at a Taiwanese tea shop such as KOI Thé, approximately 500 ml.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea, prepared with low-fat milk — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Tea, brewed (NDB 14355) and Milk, lowfat, fluid, 1% milkfat (NDB 01082), combined in approximate 60:40 ratio with added sugar at ~5 g per 100 ml
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct approved-source entry for low-fat milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy entries for brewed black tea (NDB 14355) and 1% low-fat milk (NDB 01082) in a 60:40 volumetric ratio, plus approximately 5 g sugar per 100 ml as typical for sweetened Taiwanese milk tea. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk tea estimated from analogous foods: black tea (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution) and full-fat milk tea analogues; sweetened milk tea GI estimated at 45 based on sugar and low-fat milk components per University of Sydney GI Database entries for milk (GI 27–34) and sucrose (GI 65), weighted by carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for low-fat milk tea found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low category) by weighting the GI of low-fat milk (GI ~32, University of Sydney GI Database) and sucrose (GI ~65, University of Sydney GI Database) according to their relative carbohydrate contributions in the beverage (approximately 40% from milk lactose, 60% from added sugar). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 49.0) / 100 = 22.1 g, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 9.8 × 5 = 49.0 g.

hypertension.guidance: A medium serving of low fat milk tea contains approximately 110 mg sodium, which is modest; however, individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily sodium intake and be aware that added sugar may contribute to metabolic risk factors associated with elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.guidance: A medium serving of low fat milk tea contains approximately 46 g of sugar and a glycemic load of 22, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid sweetened milk tea, request unsweetened or reduced-sugar versions, and account for the carbohydrate load within their daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sweetened low fat milk tea contributes to added sugar intake, which is associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose unsweetened or minimally sweetened versions and limit frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Fat Milk Tea (Medium) is a Taiwanese-style sweetened tea beverage prepared by combining brewed black tea with low-fat milk (approximately 1% milkfat) and sugar, served in a medium cup of approximately 500 ml. It is a staple offering at Taiwanese tea chains such as KOI Thé, which operates across Singapore and other Southeast Asian markets. The beverage has a smooth, mildly creamy texture with a balanced tea flavour and moderate sweetness. Unlike full-fat milk tea or bubble tea with tapioca pearls, this variant uses low-fat milk to reduce overall fat content while retaining the characteristic milky tea taste. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 52 kcal, 1.4 g protein, 0.5 g fat, and 9.8 g carbohydrate, predominantly from added sugar. A medium serving (500 g) delivers approximately 260 kcal and 49 g carbohydrate, making it a significant source of added sugar. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low category), but the glycemic load per serving is high at approximately 22, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The beverage contains dairy and is not suitable for lactose-intolerant or vegan consumers. It is generally considered halal. From a clinical nutrition perspective, the primary concern is the high added sugar content; consumers are advised to request reduced-sugar or sugar-free options where available.

confidence_reason: No direct approved-source entry for low-fat milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries for brewed black tea and 1% low-fat milk combined in a 60:40 ratio with typical added sugar. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for milk and sucrose. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach and absence of a direct source match.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (brewed black tea NDB 14355 and 1% low-fat milk NDB 01082) combined in a 60:40 volumetric ratio with approximately 5 g sugar per 100 ml, reflecting typical Taiwanese milk tea formulation. Energy calculated using Atwater factors (4 kcal/g protein, 9 kcal/g fat, 4 kcal/g carbohydrate). Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation; low-fat milk contains negligible trans fat and no trans fat is added). Dietary fibre is 0.0 (naturally zero; tea and milk contain no dietary fibre). Vitamin C is 0.0 (naturally zero; black tea and low-fat milk contain negligible vitamin C). Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for estimation and varies by milk fortification status in Singapore. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on weighted average of University of Sydney GI Database values for low-fat milk (GI ~32) and sucrose (GI ~65) by carbohydrate contribution. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 49.0 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 22.1. The food is sold at KOI Thé - Funan in Singapore at SGD 5.10. Actual nutrient values may vary depending on the exact sugar level selected (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) and milk-to-tea ratio used by the specific outlet. This record reflects the default standard preparation. A follow-up with HPB Singapore Foodo or direct laboratory analysis of KOI Thé low fat milk tea is recommended to improve confidence to high.
